---
title: "Diagram Examples: Mermaid, TikZ, smartdiagram"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    embed-resources: true
    code-fold: true
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    keep-tex: false
    toc: false
---

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
library(knitr)
# Global options for the knitr tikz engine.
# extra.preamble is injected into the standalone LaTeX document before \begin{document}.
# knitr compiles the tikz chunk → PDF → PNG (HTML) or embeds PDF directly (PDF output).
opts_chunk$set(
  engine.opts = list(
    extra.preamble = c(
      "\\usepackage{amsmath}",
      "\\usetikzlibrary{arrows.meta, positioning, shapes.geometric, fit, decorations.pathreplacing}",
      "\\usepackage{smartdiagram}"
    )
  )
)
```

---

## 1. Mermaid

Quarto renders Mermaid to SVG natively in HTML. For PDF, Quarto uses its bundled
mermaid-js renderer (no Chromium required in Quarto ≥ 1.4). Both formats are
supported automatically — no conditional blocks needed.

### Method-selection flowchart

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-method-flow
%%| fig-cap: "Choosing a visualization / analysis method based on number of responses and goal."
%%| fig-align: center
flowchart TD
  A([Start]) --> B{How many\nresponse variables?}
  B -->|one| C[Linear model\nCh 6–9]
  B -->|many| D{Goal?}
  D -->|inference| E[MANOVA / MLM\nCh 10–14]
  D -->|exploration| F{Structure?}
  F -->|relationships| G[PCA / Biplot\nCh 5]
  F -->|grouping| H[LDA / Discriminant\nCh 21]
```

### Analysis pipeline (left-to-right)

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-pipeline
%%| fig-cap: "Iterative analysis workflow."
%%| fig-align: center
flowchart LR
  A[Multivariate\ndata] --> B[EDA & plots]
  B --> C[Model fitting]
  C --> D[Diagnostics]
  D --> E[HE plots &\nVisualization]
  E --> F[Inference &\nReport]
  F -.->|revise model| C
```

### HE-plot concept (node diagram)

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-he-nodes
%%| fig-cap: "HE plot: the H ellipse represents the hypothesis (effect); E represents error (within-group variation)."
%%| fig-align: center
flowchart LR
  M[Fit MLM] --> H["H matrix\n(between groups)"]
  M --> E["E matrix\n(within groups)"]
  H --> HE["HE plot"]
  E --> HE
  HE --> D{"H extends\nbeyond E?"}
  D -->|yes| S[Significant\neffect]
  D -->|no| NS[Non-significant]
```

---

## 2. TikZ

The knitr `tikz` chunk engine compiles a standalone LaTeX document containing the
chunk body, then converts the output to PNG (HTML) or embeds the PDF graphic
directly (PDF output). **Both formats get the same diagram.** Libraries and packages
listed in `extra.preamble` (set in the setup chunk above) are available in every
tikz chunk.

Note: tikz chunk options use `%|` (LaTeX comment character), not `#|`.

### HE plot geometry

```{tikz}
%| label: fig-he-geom
%| fig-cap: "HE plot geometry: the H ellipse (blue) represents hypothesis variation; the E ellipse (dashed) represents error variation. When H extends beyond E the effect is significant."
%| fig-align: center
%| out-width: 75%
%| echo: true
\usetikzlibrary{arrows.meta}
\begin{tikzpicture}[>=Latex, font=\small]
  % axes
  \draw[->] (-5,0) -- (5,0) node[right] {$y_1$};
  \draw[->] (0,-2.5) -- (0,2.5) node[above] {$y_2$};
  % E ellipse (error / within-group variation)
  \draw[thick, dashed] (0,0) ellipse (2cm and 1.8cm);
  \node[above right=0.05cm and 0.1cm] at (1.8,1.6) {$\mathbf{E}$ (error)};
  % H ellipse (hypothesis / between-group variation)
  \draw[thick, blue!70!black, fill=blue!8, fill opacity=0.5]
        (0,0) ellipse (4.5cm and 0.9cm);
  \node[above right=0.05cm and 0.1cm] at (3.8, 0.75)
        {\textcolor{blue!70!black}{$\mathbf{H}$ (hypothesis)}};
  % interpretation arrow
  \draw[->, red!70!black, thick] (1.5, -2.2) -- (3.2, -0.5)
        node[midway, right=0.05cm, align=left]
        {\textcolor{red!70!black}{\small $\mathbf{H}$ extends beyond $\mathbf{E}$}\\
         \textcolor{red!70!black}{\small $\Rightarrow$ significant effect}};
\end{tikzpicture}
```

### MLM matrix equation

```{tikz}
%| label: fig-mlm-eq
%| fig-cap: "The multivariate linear model as a matrix equation."
%| fig-align: center
%| out-width: 70%
%| echo: true
\usetikzlibrary{positioning}
\begin{tikzpicture}[node distance=0.5cm and 0.3cm, font=\Large]
  \node (Y)  {$\underset{n \times p}{\mathbf{Y}}$};
  \node[right=of Y]  (eq) {$=$};
  \node[right=of eq] (X)  {$\underset{n \times q}{\mathbf{X}}$};
  \node[right=0.15cm of X] (B) {$\underset{q \times p}{\mathbf{B}}$};
  \node[right=0.4cm of B]  (pl) {$+$};
  \node[right=of pl] (E)  {$\underset{n \times p}{\mathbf{E}}$};
  % sub-labels
  \node[below=0.8cm of Y,  font=\small] {responses};
  \node[below=0.5cm of X,  font=\small, xshift=5pt] {design};
  \node[below=0.5cm of B,  font=\small, xshift=-5pt] {coefs};
  \node[below=0.8cm of E,  font=\small] {errors};
  % decorative braces
  \draw[decorate, decoration={brace, amplitude=5pt, mirror}]
    ([yshift=-4pt]X.south west) -- ([yshift=-4pt]B.south east)
    node[midway, below=8pt, font=\small] {effect};
\end{tikzpicture}
```

### Variable-relationship DAG

```{tikz}
%| label: fig-dag
%| fig-cap: "A simple DAG: predictor X and covariate Z both influence response Y."
%| fig-align: center
%| out-width: 55%
%| echo: true
\usetikzlibrary{positioning, shapes.geometric}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
    >=Latex,
    node/.style = {circle, draw, thick, minimum size=1cm, font=\large},
    lbl/.style  = {font=\small}
  ]
  \node[node] (X) at (0,0)   {$X$};
  \node[node] (Z) at (2,1.5) {$Z$};
  \node[node] (Y) at (4,0)   {$Y$};
  \draw[->, thick] (X) -- node[lbl, below] {direct} (Y);
  \draw[->, thick] (Z) -- node[lbl, above left]  {confound} (X);
  \draw[->, thick] (Z) -- node[lbl, above right] {confound} (Y);
\end{tikzpicture}
```

---

## 3. smartdiagram

`smartdiagram` provides 10 canned styles (see `issues/task-diagrams.md` §3 for
the full option reference). Loaded via `extra.preamble` in the setup chunk, it
is available in every `{tikz}` chunk. No conditional blocks needed — the knitr
engine renders PNG for HTML and a native PDF graphic for PDF output.

Options are set with `\smartdiagramset{...}` before each diagram (or once
globally). Colors can be set per-item via `set color list={...}` or uniformly
with `uniform color list=color for N items`.

There are some great examples on TeX stackExchange:

* [Hex bubble diagram](https://tex.stackexchange.com/questions/138653/how-to-make-hexagon-diagram-from-smart-diagram-package)
* [Text above arrows](https://tex.stackexchange.com/questions/469473/how-to-add-text-above-some-arrows)

### Analysis cycle — circular:clockwise

```{tikz}
%| label: fig-smart-cycle
%| fig-cap: "Statistical analysis cycle."
%| fig-align: center
%| out-width: 65%
%| echo: true
\smartdiagramset{
  circular distance=3cm,
  font=\small,
  module minimum width=1.8cm,
  module minimum height=1cm,
  text width=1.6cm,
}
\smartdiagram[circular diagram:clockwise]{
  Explore data,
  Fit model,
  Check diagnostics,
  Visualize effects,
  Report \& interpret
}
```

### TeX workflow (Kottwitz) — default styling

```{tikz}
%| label: fig-tex-workflow
%| fig-cap: "TeX document workflow (Kottwitz). See also the standalone file `test/smartdiagram-workflow.tex`."
%| fig-align: center
%| out-width: 65%
%| echo: true
\smartdiagram[circular diagram:clockwise]{Edit,
  pdf\LaTeX, Bib\TeX/ biber, make\-index, pdf\LaTeX}
```

### Book parts — horizontal flow, back arrow disabled

`back arrow disabled=true` removes the wrap-around return arrow.

```{tikz}
%| label: fig-book-flow
%| fig-cap: "Book parts as a linear progression."
%| fig-align: center
%| out-width: 95%
%| echo: true
\smartdiagramset{
  back arrow disabled=true,
  module minimum width=2cm,
  module minimum height=1.2cm,
  text width=1.8cm,
  module x sep=3,
  font=\small,
  uniform color list=teal!50 for 5 items,
}
\smartdiagram[flow diagram:horizontal]{
  Orienting\\ Ideas,
  Exploratory\\ Methods,
  Univariate\\ LM,
  Multivariate\\ LM,
  Case\\ Studies
}
```

### Chapters in a part — bubble diagram

**First item = large center bubble**; the rest orbit it as smaller overlapping
bubbles.  Works best with 4–6 satellites; more gets crowded.  Here: Part III.

```{tikz}
%| label: fig-part3-bubble
%| fig-cap: "Part III chapters as a bubble diagram. Center = part name; satellites = chapters."
%| fig-align: center
%| out-width: 70%
%| echo: true
\smartdiagramset{
  bubble center node size=3.5cm,
  bubble center node font=\small,
  bubble node size=2.2cm,
  bubble node font=\scriptsize,
  bubble fill opacity=0.45,
  bubble text opacity=0.9,
  uniform color list=teal!50 for 5 items,
  distance center/other bubbles=0.6cm,
}
\smartdiagram[bubble diagram]{
  Univariate\\ Linear\\ Models,
  Ch 6\\ Linear\\ Models,
  Ch 7\\ Model\\ Plots,
  Ch 8\\ LM\\ Topics,
  Ch 9\\ Collinearity
}
```

### Chapters in a part — constellation diagram (cleaner alternative)

Satellites are *separate* from the center with outward arrows — better when
there are many chapters or longer names.  Here: Part IV (6 chapters).

```{tikz}
%| label: fig-part4-constellation
%| fig-cap: "Part IV chapters as a constellation diagram."
%| fig-align: center
%| out-width: 75%
%| echo: true
\smartdiagramset{
  planet size=3cm,
  planet color=blue!25,
  planet font=\small,
  satellite size=1.9cm,
  satellite fill opacity=0.5,
  distance planet-satellite=3.8cm,
  font=\scriptsize,
  uniform color list=blue!20 for 7 items,
}
\smartdiagram[constellation diagram]{
  Multivariate\\ Linear Models,
  Ch 10\\ Hotelling,
  Ch 11\\ MLM Review,
  Ch 12\\ MLM Viz,
  Ch 13\\ Equal Cov,
  Ch 14\\ Influence,
  Ch 15\\ Case Studies
}
```

### Package ecosystem — connected constellation

Satellites are ring-connected too: useful to show that packages interoperate,
not just that they depend on a single hub.

```{tikz}
%| label: fig-pkg-ecosystem
%| fig-cap: "Key R packages centred on heplots, with interoperating packages as connected satellites."
%| fig-align: center
%| out-width: 65%
%| echo: true
\smartdiagramset{
  planet color=orange!60,
  planet size=2.8cm,
  planet font=\small,
  satellite size=1.8cm,
  distance planet-satellite=3.5cm,
  font=\scriptsize,
  uniform color list=orange!30 for 6 items,
  connection line width=0.08cm,
}
\smartdiagram[connected constellation diagram]{
  heplots,
  car,
  candisc,
  ggplot2,
  MASS,
  broom
}
```

### Book parts — descriptive diagram with summaries

Each item is `{Title, {Description text}}`.

```{tikz}
%| label: fig-book-descriptive
%| fig-cap: "Book parts with brief descriptions."
%| fig-align: center
%| out-width: 85%
%| echo: true
\smartdiagramset{
  set color list={blue!35, teal!40, green!35, orange!40},
  description title width=2.2cm,
  description title text width=1.8cm,
  description text width=6.5cm,
  description width=6.5cm,
  font=\small,
}
\smartdiagram[descriptive diagram]{
  {Part I,   {Orienting ideas: data ellipses and visualisation principles}},
  {Part II,  {Exploratory: PCA, biplots, and multivariate plots}},
  {Part III, {Univariate LM: model plots, collinearity, ridge regression}},
  {Part IV,  {Multivariate LM: MANOVA, HE plots, influence, robustness}},
}
```

### Sequence diagram — layered abstraction (chevron style)

`uniform sequence color` with white text gives a clean dark-band appearance.

```{tikz}
%| label: fig-sequence
%| fig-cap: "Conceptual sequence from raw data to inference."
%| fig-align: center
%| out-width: 90%
%| echo: true
\smartdiagramset{
  uniform sequence color=true,
  sequence item uniform color=teal!70!black,
  sequence item border color=black,
  sequence item font size=\small,
  sequence item text color=white,
  sequence item width=2.4cm,
  sequence item height=1.1cm,
  sequence item text width=2cm,
}
\smartdiagram[sequence diagram]{
  Raw data,
  EDA,
  LM / MLM,
  HE plots,
  Inference
}
```

---

## Notes on rendering

| Chunk type | HTML | PDF | Dependencies |
|------------|------|-----|--------------|
| `{mermaid}` | SVG (native) | PNG via Quarto renderer | none |
| `{tikz}` | PNG via knitr | PDF graphic (native) | pdflatex, pdfcrop, ghostscript/ImageMagick |
| `{=latex}` raw block | ignored | verbatim LaTeX | n/a |

Chunk option prefix by engine: `#|` for R, `%%|` for Mermaid, `%|` for TikZ.

**PDF conversion for tikz:** knitr calls `pdfcrop` (trims whitespace) then
ghostscript or ImageMagick `convert` to produce the PNG for HTML.  On Windows
check that these are on `PATH`:

```{r}
#| label: check-path
#| eval: false
Sys.which(c("pdfcrop", "gs", "convert"))   # ghostscript and ImageMagick
```
